edges.description: Loss of ARG1 catalytic function reduces terminal urea-cycle flux and elevates arginine.
ARG1 loss produces reduced or absent arginase enzyme activity, including the erythrocyte assay used diagnostically.
Hepatic ARG1 deficiency can present with cholestatic liver disease and hepatosplenomegaly in neonatal cases.
Loss of ARG1-mediated arginine hydrolysis causes marked plasma arginine elevation.
Persistent amino-acid metabolism disruption in argininemia can manifest as urinary diamino-acid elevation.
ARG1 loss elevates plasma arginine, the primary biochemical monitoring analyte.
Impaired terminal urea-cycle flux decreases conversion of arginine to urea.
Impaired ARG1 hydrolysis decreases ornithine production from arginine.
Impaired ureagenesis can produce intermittent ammonia elevation during catabolic stress.
ARG1-D causes infrequent but clinically relevant hyperammonemic episodes.
In severe ARG1-D, intermittent hyperammonemia can progress to acute hyperammonemic coma, captured clinically as encephalopathy.
Persistent hyperargininemia in ARG1-D is associated with chronic growth failure in the core clinical syndrome.
Chronic arginine, ammonia, and guanidino-compound accumulation acts as neurotoxic exposure.
ARG1-D causes accumulation of arginine metabolites, including guanidino compounds.
Increased arginine availability can perturb arginine partitioning into nitric oxide and related pathways.
Chronic metabolite neurotoxicity contributes to neuronal damage and downstream white matter pathology.
Metabolite neurotoxicity underlies the progressive spastic paraparesis hallmark of ARG1-D.
Progressive neurologic toxicity produces treatable pediatric spasticity.
Accumulated neurotoxic metabolites contribute to seizures in hyperargininemia.
Chronic metabolite neurotoxicity contributes to progressive intellectual impairment.
Chronic metabolite neurotoxicity can produce severe intellectual disability in argininemia.
Chronic metabolite neurotoxicity contributes to developmental delay and cognitive impairment.
Progressive neurologic dysfunction can include abnormal speech and vocalization.
Neurotoxicity and seizure susceptibility can be accompanied by abnormal EEG findings.
Progressive neurologic toxicity can evolve to severe generalized spastic motor impairment.
Progressive neurologic involvement in ARG1 deficiency can include microcephaly.
White matter lesions and abnormal white-matter imaging are captured as abnormal cerebral white matter morphology.
White matter and corticospinal tract pathology contributes to progressive spastic paraparesis.
Hyperammonemic crises can present with acute encephalopathy or coma.
ARG1-deficient patients with hyperargininemia exhibit persistent growth retardation.
Increased plasma arginine directly reports the terminal urea-cycle block and hyperargininemia mechanism in ARG1 deficiency.
Falling plasma arginine reports pharmacodynamic restoration of arginine disposal with pegzilarginase enzyme therapy.
Increased guanidino compounds report the neurotoxic metabolite branch downstream of chronic hyperargininemia.
Decreased ornithine and an increased arginine-to-ornithine ratio report the impaired ARG1 hydrolysis reaction.
The arginine-to-ornithine ratio improves newborn-screening discrimination for hyperargininemia.
Increased ammonia reports intermittent hyperammonemic episodes in ARG1 deficiency, especially during catabolic stress.
Reduced erythrocyte arginase activity functionally reports the ARG1 molecular-function deficiency.
Decreased urea production reports impaired terminal urea-cycle flux from loss of ARG1-catalyzed arginine hydrolysis.

nodes.description: Biallelic pathogenic ARG1 variants reduce arginase catalytic activity in hepatocytes.

evidence.snippet: ARG1-deficient patients exhibit hyperargininemia with spastic paraparesis, progressive neurological and intellectual impairment, persistent growth retardation, and infrequent episodes of hyperammonemia, a clinical pattern that differs strikingly from other urea cycle disorders.

This genetic disorder is caused by 40+ mutations found fairly uniformly spread throughout the ARG1 gene, resulting in partial or complete loss of enzyme function, which catalyzes the hydrolysis of arginine to ornithine and urea.
ARG1 | HGNC:663 | hyperargininemia | MONDO:0008814 | AR | Definitive

nodes.description: Neuroimaging abnormalities including white matter lesions, altered white-matter MRS metabolite patterns, cerebral atrophy, and cerebellar atrophy have been reported in hyperargininemia.

evidence.reference_title: A long-term survival case of arginase deficiency with severe multicystic white matter and compound mutations.
evidence.snippet: The magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of our case showed severe multicystic white matter lesions with cortical atrophy

We observed mild cerebral and cerebellar atrophy and infarct at bilateral posterior putamen and insular cortex localization on conventional images and elevated choline/creatine ratios and abnormal peak at 3.8 ppm

nodes.description: Abnormal speech or vocalization is a very frequent neurologic feature in Orphanet's argininemia record.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002167 | Abnormality of speech or vocalization | Very frequent (99-80%)

ARG1-deficient patients exhibit hyperargininemia with spastic paraparesis, progressive neurological and intellectual impairment, persistent growth retardation, and infrequent episodes of hyperammonemia, a clinical pattern that differs strikingly from other urea cycle disorders.

readouts.interpretation: Increased ammonia reports intermittent hyperammonemic episodes in ARG1 deficiency, especially during catabolic stress.

nodes.description: Seizure management with appropriate antiepileptic drugs, given that seizures occur in 60-75% of patients. Seizure control is an important aspect of supportive care.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and neurologic progression of hyperargininemia.
evidence.snippet: Seizures in hyperargininemia may be more common than reported in previous studies.
Agents/circumstances to avoid: Valproic acid (exacerbates hyperammonemia).

nodes.description: Symptomatic treatment of spasticity with physical therapy, botulinum toxin injections, and oral antispasticity agents to maintain mobility and prevent contractures. Early intervention is important given the progressive nature of the motor involvement.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and neurologic progression of hyperargininemia.
evidence.snippet: Hyperargininemia is one of the few treatable causes of pediatric spastic paraparesis, and can be confused with cerebral palsy.

nodes.description: Arginine is a shared substrate for arginase and nitric oxide synthase (NOS). Loss of ARG1 activity shifts arginine partitioning and may perturb nitric oxide and polyamine biosynthesis, with implications for vascular function and cellular signaling. ARG1 is also expressed in erythrocytes and immune cells, suggesting broader metabolic consequences beyond the hepatic urea cycle.

evidence.snippet: Finally, some of the functions and implications of ARG1 in non-urea cycle activities are considered.

nodes.description: White matter lesions, altered white-matter MRS patterns, and cortical or cerebellar atrophy are modeled as central nervous system substrates for progressive spasticity in ARG1 deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: A long-term survival case of arginase deficiency with severe multicystic white matter and compound mutations.
evidence.snippet: The magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of our case showed severe multicystic white matter lesions with cortical atrophy

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and neurologic progression of hyperargininemia.
evidence.snippet: Progressive spastic diplegia constituted the key clinical abnormality in this group, but variability in clinical presentation and progression were evident in our series.

nodes.description: Diaminoaciduria is a very frequent urinary biochemical phenotype in Orphanet's argininemia profile.

evidence.snippet: HP:0008339 | Diaminoaciduria | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: EEG abnormalities are frequent in the Orphanet argininemia phenotype profile.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002353 | EEG abnormality | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Acute or recurrent encephalopathy may occur during hyperammonemic crises triggered by catabolic stress, though this is less common than in other urea cycle disorders.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and neurologic progression of hyperargininemia.
evidence.snippet: Hyperargininemia is an autosomal recessive metabolic disorder caused by a deficiency of enzyme arginase I. It is a rare pan-ethnic disease with a clinical presentation distinct from that of other urea cycle disorders, and hyperammonemic encephalopathy is not usually observed.

evidence.reference_title: Cluster of Severe Arginase 1 Deficiency in the Comoros: Clinical, Neuroimaging, and Molecular Features in 17 Patients From Mayotte Compared With 10 From Paris.
evidence.snippet: The main clinical features at diagnosis included hyperammonemic coma (with or without liver failure) and neurodevelopmental delay with spastic diplegia.

This genetic disorder is caused by 40+ mutations found fairly uniformly spread throughout the ARG1 gene, resulting in partial or complete loss of enzyme function, which catalyzes the hydrolysis of arginine to ornithine and urea.

readouts.interpretation: Reduced erythrocyte arginase activity functionally reports the ARG1 molecular-function deficiency.

nodes.description: Genetic counseling for affected families including discussion of autosomal recessive inheritance, 25% recurrence risk, carrier testing, and prenatal diagnostic options. Carrier screening implications are increasingly recognized given the expanding understanding of ARG1 variant pathogenicity.

evidence.snippet: Arginase-1 (ARG1) deficiency is a rare autosomal recessive disorder that affects the liver-based urea cycle, leading to impaired ureagenesis.
GENETIC COUNSELING: Arginase deficiency is inherited in an autosomal recessive manner.

nodes.description: Developmental delay is common, often preceding the recognition of spastic paraparesis and intellectual disability. Motor milestones may be delayed or lost as the disease progresses.

evidence.reference_title: Argininemia: Pathophysiology and Novel Methods for Evaluation of the Disease
evidence.snippet: The disease is associated with progressive development of spasticity and other symptoms, including seizures, developmental delay, cognitive impairment, and hepatic pathology.

nodes.description: Persistent growth retardation is a characteristic feature, likely reflecting chronic catabolic stress and dietary protein restriction. Short stature is observed in the majority of patients.

evidence.snippet: ARG1-deficient patients exhibit hyperargininemia with spastic paraparesis, progressive neurological and intellectual impairment, persistent growth retardation, and infrequent episodes of hyperammonemia, a clinical pattern that differs strikingly from other urea cycle disorders.

evidence.reference_title: Argininemia: Pathophysiology and Novel Methods for Evaluation of the Disease
evidence.snippet: Different factors, such as the accumulation of arginine, ammonia, and guanidino compounds, act as neurotoxins and may account for the neurological sequelae observed in the disease.

readouts.interpretation: Increased guanidino compounds report the neurotoxic metabolite branch downstream of chronic hyperargininemia.

nodes.description: Hepatic involvement including hepatomegaly or hepatosplenomegaly may occur, particularly in rare neonatal cholestatic presentations of ARG1 deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Neonatal cholestasis: an uncommon presentation of hyperargininemia.
evidence.snippet: In case 1, diagnosis was established at 2 months of age upon investigation of the etiology of cholestatic injury pattern and hepatosplenomegaly

nodes.description: Episodic hyperammonemia occurs during catabolic stress, but is notably less frequent than in other urea cycle disorders. This distinguishing feature reflects the unique position of ARG1 as the terminal urea cycle enzyme with partial compensatory activity from ARG2.

evidence.snippet: ARG1-deficient patients exhibit hyperargininemia with spastic paraparesis, progressive neurological and intellectual impairment, persistent growth retardation, and infrequent episodes of hyperammonemia, a clinical pattern that differs strikingly from other urea cycle disorders.

evidence.reference_title: Efficacy and safety of pegzilarginase in arginase 1 deficiency (PEACE): a phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multi-centre trial.
evidence.snippet: Arginase 1 Deficiency (ARG1-D) is a rare debilitating, progressive, inherited, metabolic disease characterized by marked increases in plasma arginine (pArg) and its metabolites, with increased morbidity, substantial reductions in quality of life, and premature mortality.

nodes.description: Persistent elevation of plasma arginine, often exceeding 300 umol/L, is the defining biochemical feature. Plasma arginine levels are the primary biomarker for disease monitoring and therapeutic response.

evidence.reference_title: Efficacy and safety of pegzilarginase in arginase 1 deficiency (PEACE): a phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multi-centre trial.
evidence.snippet: Arginase 1 Deficiency (ARG1-D) is a rare debilitating, progressive, inherited, metabolic disease characterized by marked increases in plasma arginine (pArg) and its metabolites, with increased morbidity, substantial reductions in quality of life, and premature mortality.

ARG1-deficient patients exhibit hyperargininemia with spastic paraparesis, progressive neurological and intellectual impairment, persistent growth retardation, and infrequent episodes of hyperammonemia, a clinical pattern that differs strikingly from other urea cycle disorders.

nodes.description: Loss of ARG1 activity causes persistent hyperargininemia with plasma arginine levels exceeding 300 umol/L, impaired nitrogen disposal, and intermittent hyperammonemia during catabolic stress. Unlike other urea cycle disorders, hyperammonemia is relatively infrequent, and the dominant disease driver is chronic arginine toxicity.

evidence.reference_title: Efficacy and safety of pegzilarginase in arginase 1 deficiency (PEACE): a phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multi-centre trial.
evidence.snippet: Arginase 1 Deficiency (ARG1-D) is a rare debilitating, progressive, inherited, metabolic disease characterized by marked increases in plasma arginine (pArg) and its metabolites, with increased morbidity, substantial reductions in quality of life, and premature mortality.

nodes.description: Progressive intellectual impairment is a common feature, often developing in early childhood alongside motor deterioration. The cognitive decline is attributed to chronic neurotoxicity from accumulated arginine and guanidino compounds.

evidence.snippet: ARG1-deficient patients exhibit hyperargininemia with spastic paraparesis, progressive neurological and intellectual impairment, persistent growth retardation, and infrequent episodes of hyperammonemia, a clinical pattern that differs strikingly from other urea cycle disorders.

nodes.description: Liver transplantation can restore hepatic urea cycle function and halt neurological deterioration. It is considered for patients with severe metabolic instability refractory to medical management.
Microcephaly has been reported as part of the progressive neurological syndrome in some patients with ARG1 deficiency; the precise causal intermediate is uncertain.

evidence.reference_title: Lethal phenotype in conditional late-onset arginase 1 deficiency in the mouse.
evidence.snippet: It usually presents with an insidious onset and manifests with a progressive neurological syndrome with microcephaly, spasticity, seizures, clonus, spastic diplegia, and failure to thrive

nodes.description: Accumulation of arginine and its downstream guanidino compound derivatives contributes to neurotoxicity through multiple mechanisms including inhibition of GABAergic and glycinergic neurotransmission, disruption of Na+/K+-ATPase activity, and induction of oxidative stress in brain tissue. These neurotoxic effects underlie the progressive neurological deterioration characteristic of the disease.

evidence.snippet: This review briefly highlights the current understanding of the etiology and pathophysiology of ARG1 deficiency derived from clinical case reports and therapeutic strategies stretching over several decades and reports on several exciting new developments regarding the pathophysiology of the disorder using ARG1 global and inducible knockout mouse models.

evidence.reference_title: Argininemia: Pathophysiology and Novel Methods for Evaluation of the Disease
evidence.snippet: Different factors, such as the accumulation of arginine, ammonia, and guanidino compounds, act as neurotoxins and may account for the neurological sequelae observed in the disease.

nodes.description: Arginase deficiency is detectable by newborn screening using tandem mass spectrometry with elevated arginine as the primary marker. Use of the arginine-to-ornithine ratio improves sensitivity and specificity, as neonatal arginine levels may appear near-normal due to maternal arginase or ARG2 activity.

evidence.reference_title: Newborn screening for hyperargininemia due to arginase 1 deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Improved newborn screening effectiveness should lead to better case detection and more rapid treatment to lower plasma arginine levels hence improving long term outcome of individuals with hyperargininemia.

nodes.description: Nitrogen scavengers including sodium benzoate, sodium phenylbutyrate, and sodium phenylacetate are used to provide alternative pathways for nitrogen excretion, particularly during hyperammonemic episodes and as chronic adjunctive therapy.

evidence.snippet: This genetic disorder is caused by 40+ mutations found fairly uniformly spread throughout the ARG1 gene, resulting in partial or complete loss of enzyme function, which catalyzes the hydrolysis of arginine to ornithine and urea.

readouts.interpretation: Decreased ornithine and an increased arginine-to-ornithine ratio report the impaired ARG1 hydrolysis reaction.
The arginine-to-ornithine ratio improves newborn-screening discrimination for hyperargininemia.

nodes.description: Pegzilarginase is a pegylated recombinant human arginase 1 enzyme therapy that represents the first potential disease-modifying treatment for ARG1-D. In the Phase 3 PEACE trial, weekly pegzilarginase reduced geometric mean plasma arginine from 354 to 86 umol/L at 24 weeks, normalizing levels in 90.5% of patients compared to 0% with placebo, with clinically relevant functional mobility improvements.

evidence.reference_title: Efficacy and safety of pegzilarginase in arginase 1 deficiency (PEACE): a phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multi-centre trial.
evidence.snippet: In addition, clinically relevant functional mobility improvements were demonstrated with pegzilarginase treatment. These effects were sustained long-term through additional 24 weeks of subsequent exposure.

evidence.reference_title: Efficacy and safety of pegzilarginase in arginase 1 deficiency (PEACE): a phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, multi-centre trial.
evidence.snippet: Arginase 1 Deficiency (ARG1-D) is a rare debilitating, progressive, inherited, metabolic disease characterized by marked increases in plasma arginine (pArg) and its metabolites

readouts.interpretation: Increased plasma arginine directly reports the terminal urea-cycle block and hyperargininemia mechanism in ARG1 deficiency.
Falling plasma arginine reports pharmacodynamic restoration of arginine disposal with pegzilarginase enzyme therapy.

nodes.description: Progressive generalized spastic motor impairment is recorded by Orphanet as a frequent phenotype.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002478 | Progressive spastic quadriplegia | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Dietary protein restriction with supplementation of essential amino acids is the cornerstone of chronic management. The primary goal is to reduce plasma arginine concentration below 200 umol/L, though this target is rarely achievable with diet alone due to endogenous arginine production.
Seizures occur in approximately 60-75% of patients and are linked to the epileptogenic properties of accumulated guanidino compounds. Seizure frequency may be more common than historically reported.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and neurologic progression of hyperargininemia.
evidence.snippet: Seizures in hyperargininemia may be more common than reported in previous studies.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and neurologic progression of hyperargininemia.
evidence.snippet: Hyperargininemia is an autosomal recessive metabolic disorder caused by a deficiency of enzyme arginase I. It is a rare pan-ethnic disease with a clinical presentation distinct from that of other urea cycle disorders, and hyperammonemic encephalopathy is not usually observed.

nodes.description: Severe intellectual disability is listed as very frequent in the Orphanet argininemia record.

evidence.snippet: HP:0010864 | Intellectual disability, severe | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Progressive spastic diplegia or paraparesis is the hallmark neurological feature, typically presenting in early childhood. Upper motor neuron involvement occurs in approximately 80% of patients, often initially mistaken for cerebral palsy.

evidence.snippet: ARG1-deficient patients exhibit hyperargininemia with spastic paraparesis, progressive neurological and intellectual impairment, persistent growth retardation, and infrequent episodes of hyperammonemia, a clinical pattern that differs strikingly from other urea cycle disorders.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and neurologic progression of hyperargininemia.
evidence.snippet: Progressive spastic diplegia constituted the key clinical abnormality in this group, but variability in clinical presentation and progression were evident in our series.

nodes.description: Upper and lower extremity spasticity is a predominant motor finding, with corticospinal tract involvement. This is the feature most often leading to initial clinical evaluation and may be mistaken for cerebral palsy.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and neurologic progression of hyperargininemia.
evidence.snippet: Hyperargininemia is one of the few treatable causes of pediatric spastic paraparesis, and can be confused with cerebral palsy.
Arginase-1 (ARG1) deficiency is a rare autosomal recessive disorder that affects the liver-based urea cycle, leading to impaired ureagenesis.

readouts.interpretation: Decreased urea production reports impaired terminal urea-cycle flux from loss of ARG1-catalyzed arginine hydrolysis.

nodes.description: More than 40 pathogenic mutations have been identified across the ARG1 gene on chromosome 6q23, distributed fairly uniformly throughout the gene. Variants include missense, nonsense, frameshift, and splice-site mutations resulting in partial or complete loss of enzyme function.
